fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build out that test linting library we've been talking about. Basically the idea is that devs paste in their test source code and a config of which checks they want enabled, and the tool spits out a structured report of problems. Think of it like the same pattern we used for the import validator we shipped last quarter — same kind of adapter wrapping the core logic.

The output needs to be consistent and machine-readable so CI pipelines can consume it. Each problem should say which rule fired, a human-readable message, where in the file it is (line and column), and how bad it is. Some checks also need to support auto-fixing, where the tool emits the corrected source alongside the diagnostics.

We also need to handle that weird snapshot preprocessing flow — someone on the test infra team said there's a two-phase thing where you preprocess a snapshot file and then postprocess a message array to filter it down. That part is a bit fuzzy to me honestly, just make sure it works like the existing snapshot tooling expects.

The checks we need cover things like accidentally committed skip/focus flags, tests with no real assertions, broken async assertion patterns, bad test titles, messy hook setup, wrong import patterns, and a few more. The rules should be individually toggleable. Severity should map to numbers the way our other linting tools do it. Please keep the core logic totally separate from how input/output is handled.

A couple more specifics from the questions that came back. On the output shape, we need stdout to be newline-delimited key=value pairs in this exact order: first an optional `output=<fixed source>` line only when a fix was applied, then `messages=<count>`, then for each diagnostic in zero-based index order: `messageN.rule`, `messageN.text`, `messageN.line`, `messageN.column`, `messageN.severity`. No extra blank lines, no JSON, no other formatting. For severity, emit integers only: 'error' maps to severity=2 and 'warn' maps to severity=1, so the line needs to be exactly `messageN.severity=2` or `messageN.severity=1`, not the words themselves.

Also, all rule identifiers in the output need the `jest/` prefix. So even if the incoming rules config uses the short name like `"no-disabled-tests": "error"`, the emitted diagnostic should look like `message0.rule=jest/no-disabled-tests`. Same idea for the rest of the rules.

A few rule-specific details too. For `no-duplicate-hooks`, flag the second occurrence, not the first, and report the column where that duplicate hook call starts. In `describe("suite", () => { beforeEach(() => {}); beforeEach(() => {}) })`, the second `beforeEach` starts at column=49, so that is what gets reported. For `lowercase-name`, the column should point to the opening quote of the title string argument, not the start of the `describe` or `it` call. In `describe("FOO", () => {})`, the string `"FOO"` starts at column=10, so the diagnostic reports column=10. And for `no-mocks-import`, the message needs to be exactly `Mocks should not be manually imported from a __mocks__ directory. Instead use jest.mock and import from the original module path.` and it should fire whenever an import path contains `__mocks__`.

On the architecture side, yes, this should follow the adapter pattern I mentioned before: a thin I/O adapter (stdin JSON → core API calls → stdout key=value) wrapping a pure domain engine, as seen in feature9_import_export_restrictions.json and the overall adapter design in start.md. The execution adapter is responsible for handling stdin/stdout and JSON parsing, and it needs to stay strictly separated from the core linting engine. The core domain module should expose an idiomatic programmatic API, like a `lint()` function or `Linter` class, and it should not import or reference any I/O modules. The adapter just translates JSON commands into calls to that core API and formats the results into the key=value output format.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: lint(source: string, rules: Record<string, string>, options?: { fix?: boolean }): LintResult

trigger_keywords: column numbering
is column 0-indexed or 1-indexed
column=1 for first character
column offset
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Both line and column numbers in the output are 1-indexed. The very first character of the source string is at line=1, column=1. For example, `it.skip(...)` at the start of input must report column=1, while `describe.only(...)` must report the `.only` member expression position which starts at column=10 (the dot before 'only').

trigger_keywords: fix output line
when is output= emitted
auto-fix behavior
fixed source in output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The `output=<fixed source>` line is only emitted when the input includes `"fix": true` AND at least one applicable fix was performed. The fixed source replaces the entire original source on that single line. If fix is false or absent, or if no fixable rule fired, the output= line must be omitted entirely. The output= line must appear before the messages= line.

trigger_keywords: snapshot mode
preprocess snapshot
postprocess messages
two-phase snapshot handling
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
Snapshot handling uses a separate mode indicated by `"mode": "snapshot"`. For `"action": "preprocess"`, the input has a `source` field containing snapshot file text, and output must be `part0=<snapshot source>
`. For `"action": "postprocess"`, the input has a `messages` array of message arrays, and output must list only the messages whose ruleId starts with `jest/`, formatted as `messages=<count>
` followed by `messageN.rule=<ruleId>
` lines.

trigger_keywords: valid-expect two messages
expect with no arguments reports twice
missing matcher and missing argument
order of valid-expect diagnostics
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When `valid-expect` fires on `expect()` with no arguments and no matcher, it must emit exactly two diagnostics in this order: first the missing-matcher diagnostic at the column of `expect` (column=20 in the test case), then the missing-argument diagnostic at the column of the empty argument list / closing paren of expect (column=26 in the test case). Both carry rule=jest/valid-expect and severity=2.

trigger_keywords: no-alias-methods canonical names
toBeCalled replacement
alias method mapping
what matchers are aliases
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The `no-alias-methods` rule maps alias matcher names to their canonical equivalents. At minimum: `toBeCalled` → `toHaveBeenCalled`, `toBeCalledWith` → `toHaveBeenCalledWith`, `toHaveBeenCalledTimes` is canonical (not an alias). The diagnostic message format is: `Replace <alias>() with its canonical name of <canonical>()`. The column reported is the column of the alias matcher name in the source, not the start of the expect call.

trigger_keywords: require-top-level-describe rule
test must be inside describe
top level it or test
describe wrapper requirement
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The `require-top-level-describe` rule flags any `it`, `test`, or `fit` call that is not nested inside a `describe` block. The message must be: `All test cases must be wrapped in a describe block.` at line=1, column=1 for a top-level test. A test correctly nested inside `describe(...)` produces messages=0.

trigger_keywords: no-test-callback done parameter
callback style test column
done argument detection
where is the column for no-test-callback
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The `no-test-callback` rule fires when a test callback function declares a `done` parameter. The column reported must point to the `done` parameter position in the source, not the start of the `it` call. In the test case `it("case", done => { done() })`, column=12 corresponds to the position of the `done` parameter in the callback.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid action field values
what values can action take
list all supported action values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which action strings does the adapter support
what are the possible action inputs
tell me all the action names
what operations are supported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'action' field. Valid values are: 'preprocess', 'postprocess'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: that weird snapshot preprocessing flow — someone on the test infra team said there's a two-phase thing
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The snapshot mode described in feature11_snapshot_handling.json: mode=snapshot with action=preprocess (returns part0=<source>) and action=postprocess (filters a messages array to only jest/ prefixed rules and returns count + rule names)

context_pointers.vague_reference: the way our other linting tools do it
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Severity is emitted as an integer (2 for error, 1 for warn) in the output field messageN.severity, matching ESLint's numeric severity convention as shown consistently across all feature test case expected_output fields

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect. Please check the test case files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for the exact expected input/output contract, and refer to the Architecture & Engineering Constraints section of start.md for structural requirements. When in doubt, the test cases are the authoritative specification for output format.
